content.text: The distinction between an AI agent and a workflow is the difference between a system that
its next step at runtime and one that
a step the developer decided at design time. Both use LLMs; both can call tools. The difference lies entirely in who controls the execution graph: the model or the programmer.

(also called a pipeline) is a fixed, deterministic sequence of steps defined in code. Each step may call an LLM, but the order, branching conditions, and exit criteria are all hard-coded by the developer. Given the same input, a workflow takes the same path every time — conditionals included, because the conditionals themselves are code.

uses an LLM to control the execution graph at runtime. The developer defines the available tools and the starting context, but the model decides which tool to call next, what arguments to pass, and when to stop. Given the same input on two separate runs, the agent may take different paths — because the LLM's output is not deterministic.
The boundary is sharper than it first appears:
A workflow that calls an LLM to classify intent and then routes to one of three fixed branches is
— the LLM is just a component inside a developer-controlled graph.
A system where the LLM decides whether to search, whether to summarize, and whether to stop is

The distinction drives nearly every practical engineering decision: cost structure, testability, reliability, latency, and the complexity of the failure modes. Choosing incorrectly — building an agent when a workflow would do, or building a workflow for a task that genuinely requires dynamic adaptation — leads to systems that are either unnecessarily expensive and unpredictable, or brittle and unmaintainable.

Workflows fail in a predictable, diagnosable way: a specific step produces wrong output, and fixing it means fixing that step. Agents fail in emergent ways: the model made a plausible but wrong sequence of decisions, and the bug is in the interaction between steps, not any single step. Workflows are easy to unit test; agents require trace-level evaluation and evals that assess multi-step trajectories.

The architectural choice also determines what can go wrong at scale. A workflow with a bug in step 3 produces the same wrong output every run — the failure is contained and consistent. An agent with a reasoning error produces wrong outputs on some runs and correct outputs on others — the failure is intermittent and harder to detect.
The same input produces the same execution path every time, making testing, monitoring, and SLA guarantees straightforward.
The number of LLM calls is fixed, so cost per task is calculable at design time.
Failures are isolated to a specific step. The call stack tells you exactly where things went wrong.
No overhead from the model deciding what to do next — the orchestration layer moves immediately to the next step.
When the steps required vary by input or depend on what is discovered during execution, a fixed workflow cannot adapt; an agent can.
When a tool call fails or returns unexpected data, the model can revise its plan. A workflow would proceed to the next step regardless.
For complex multi-domain tasks, the developer does not need to enumerate every possible execution path — the model handles branching.
Adding a new tool makes it immediately available to the agent without modifying the orchestration code.
Every deviation from the happy path requires a developer to update the code. For tasks with many edge cases, the workflow grows brittle over time.
Cost and latency vary per run; the model may call five tools or fifteen for the same task on different runs.
Unit tests mock individual steps. Integration tests follow the fixed path. Agent testing requires trace-level evaluation across many sampled runs.
A workflow has a defined end; an agent requires an explicit step limit and termination condition, or it may run indefinitely.

A workflow is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) — or occasionally a graph with explicit loops — where nodes are processing steps and edges are control-flow transitions. The transitions are either unconditional (always go to step N+1) or conditional on the output of the current step (if classification == "complaint" go to step C, else go to step B). Every possible path through the graph is enumerated by the developer.

LLM calls in a workflow are leaf-node operations: they receive a constructed prompt and return structured output (often parsed into a typed object). The LLM does not control what happens next — the workflow's routing logic does.

An agent's execution graph is not defined in advance. At each iteration, the LLM receives the full context — the original task, the history of all previous tool calls and their results, and the list of available tools — and generates either a tool call (specifying which tool to invoke and with what arguments) or a final answer. The orchestration layer executes the tool call and feeds the result back as the next observation. This continues until the model signals completion.

The model's output at each step is both the "work product" and the "routing decision". This is the fundamental difference from a workflow: in a workflow, routing and work are separate concerns handled by separate code; in an agent, the model handles both simultaneously.
The diagram below contrasts the two execution models. A workflow follows fixed arrows; an agent's LLM core decides dynamically which tools to call (dashed arrows, labeled "maybe").

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison: workflow with fixed sequential steps vs agent with LLM core dynamically selecting tools

content.text: Use a workflow when the task has a known, bounded set of steps that are unlikely to change based on runtime data. Use an agent when the task is open-ended, the number of steps varies, or self-correction based on intermediate results is required.
Summarize a PDF in 3 bullet points
One LLM call, no tool use needed
Research a topic, citing current sources
Debug a failing test in a codebase
Path depends on what errors are found
Each ticket requires different tool combinations
The structural difference shows up clearly in code. Both examples below accomplish the same surface-level task — answer a question using web search — but with fundamentally different control flow.

Both implementations produce an answer to the same question. The workflow always uses exactly two LLM calls and one search; the agent may use zero, one, or several of each depending on what the model determines it needs. The workflow is cheaper and faster for this simple case; the agent handles follow-up complexity the workflow cannot.
A retrieval-augmented generation pipeline is a classic workflow: embed the query, retrieve the top-k documents, format a prompt, generate an answer. The steps never change regardless of the question.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Fixed pipeline: embed → retrieve → generate (always exactly these 3 steps)
def rag_pipeline(question: str, vector_db) -> str:
    # Step 1: embed
    embedding = get_embedding(question)           # deterministic
    # Step 2: retrieve
    docs = vector_db.search(embedding, top_k=5)  # deterministic
    context = "\n\n".join(d.text for d in docs)
    # Step 3: generate
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"
        }]
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

# get_embedding() is a placeholder for any embedding API
def get_embedding(text: str) -> list[float]:
    return [0.0] * 1536  # stub

The same question about a technical topic might require one search for a simple fact, or five searches plus page fetches for a nuanced comparison. A workflow cannot handle that variance; an agent adapts.
A SaaS product ships two AI features side by side, each chosen deliberately for its architecture:

is a workflow: extract text (OCR), call LLM to extract structured fields (vendor, amount, date, line items), validate against schema, write to database. Every invoice runs the same four steps. Cost is $0.003 per invoice, latency is 4 seconds, and accuracy is 97%. The team unit tests each step independently.

is an agent: given a list of unmatched expenses and access to a database query tool, an email search tool, and a policy lookup tool, it autonomously searches for matching records, reads policy documents when an expense is ambiguous, and drafts a reconciliation report. Some expenses reconcile in two tool calls; others require eight. The team cannot write a workflow for this because the path through the data depends on what each query returns.

Both features are in production. The workflow handles 500k invoices per month at predictable cost. The agent handles 2k reconciliation sessions per month at variable cost ($0.05–$0.40 per session) with human review for sessions flagged by the agent as requiring judgment. Neither choice was wrong — each matched the task's structure.
Mistake 1 — Building an agent when a workflow would do

If the task always requires the same three steps in the same order, adding an agent loop introduces variable cost, non-deterministic behavior, and a harder-to-test system with no benefit. The decision criterion is not "could an agent do this?" but "does the task genuinely require dynamic step selection?".

# Unnecessary agent — this task has exactly one step
def summarize_text_agent(text: str) -> str:
    # Wrapping in an agent loop adds zero value here
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": f"Summarize: {text}"}]
    for _ in range(10):
        resp = call_llm_with_tools(messages, tools=[])
        if resp.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            return resp.content[0].text
        ...

# Correct: just call the LLM directly
def summarize_text(text: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Summarize in 3 sentences: {text}"}]
    )
    return resp.content[0].text
Mistake 2 — Adding conditional logic to an agent to "guide" it

If code outside the agent loop checks tool results and decides what the agent should do next, the system is a workflow that happens to call a loop API. The "agent" becomes a fiction and the developer is carrying the orchestration burden anyway. Commit to one model: either the code controls the path (workflow) or the LLM does (agent).
Mistake 3 — Treating agent non-determinism as a bug

Developers accustomed to workflows often add code to constrain which tools the agent can call on each iteration, or to force a specific tool sequence, in order to make the agent "deterministic". This reproduces a workflow with extra steps. If determinism is required, use a workflow. If the task genuinely needs dynamic adaptation, accept and test for the non-determinism.
For any new AI feature, start with the simplest implementation: a single LLM call or a fixed pipeline. Only add an agent loop when workflow limitations create a real problem.
Before choosing, ask: "Does the number or type of steps vary based on what the task discovers at runtime?" If yes, agent. If no, workflow.

The outer structure is often a workflow (receive request → process → respond) while one step in the workflow is an agent sub-task (the processing step). This localizes the non-determinism to the step where it adds value.
Measure agent step counts in production.
If p95 of your agent runs complete in 3 steps and the max is 4, the task is probably a workflow in disguise. Collapse it — you'll gain predictability and lower costs.
Test workflow steps with unit tests (mock the LLM, assert the output). Evaluate agents with trace-based evals that score the full trajectory of decisions across many sampled runs.

Workflows have fixed, predictable cost profiles. A 3-step pipeline with known max token counts can be budgeted precisely: cost per run = sum of (tokens × price) across steps. At 100k runs per day, total daily cost is deterministic.

Agent cost is a distribution. A task that takes between 3 and 12 steps has a cost distribution that may have a long tail — a few runs take 10× longer than the median and consume 10× the tokens. At scale, budget for the p95 or p99 cost, not the median.

Throughput characteristics also differ. Workflows scale horizontally with trivial coordination: spin up more workers, each runs the same fixed pipeline independently. Agent throughput is bounded by per-agent step depth — parallel agents can run concurrently, but each individual agent run is sequential by nature (each step depends on the previous tool result).
Both architectures have dedicated tooling support:

Prefect, Airflow, Temporal, AWS Step Functions, and LangChain's LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) all model fixed pipelines with LLM step support. LangGraph's StateGraph supports both workflows (explicit node transitions) and agents (model-controlled routing).
LangGraph ReAct agent, LlamaIndex AgentRunner, CrewAI, AutoGen, and the native tool-use APIs of Anthropic and OpenAI all implement the perceive–reason–act loop.

LangSmith, Langfuse, and Arize Phoenix provide trace-level visualization for both. For agents, trace views are especially important — they show the full decision trajectory, not just individual LLM calls.

# LangGraph: same graph framework, two routing styles
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

# --- Workflow style: explicit transitions ---
def workflow_graph():
    builder = StateGraph(dict)
    builder.add_node("step_a", lambda s: {"a_result": process_a(s["input"])})
    builder.add_node("step_b", lambda s: {"b_result": process_b(s["a_result"])})
    builder.add_node("step_c", lambda s: {"output": finalize(s["b_result"])})
    builder.set_entry_point("step_a")
    builder.add_edge("step_a", "step_b")  # fixed
    builder.add_edge("step_b", "step_c")  # fixed
    builder.add_edge("step_c", END)
    return builder.compile()

# --- Agent style: model controls routing ---
def agent_graph():
    builder = StateGraph(dict)
    builder.add_node("llm", call_model_with_tools)   # decides next step
    builder.add_node("tools", execute_tools)
    builder.set_entry_point("llm")
    # Conditional routing: model says "done" → END; else → tools → llm
    builder.add_conditional_edges(
        "llm",
        lambda s: END if s.get("done") else "tools"
    )
    builder.add_edge("tools", "llm")  # always loop back
    return builder.compile()

def process_a(x): return x
def process_b(x): return x
def finalize(x): return x
def call_model_with_tools(s): return s
def execute_tools(s): return s

Workflows and agents have different failure signatures. A workflow fails at a specific, identifiable step — the failure is local and often recoverable by retrying that step. An agent can fail in ways that are causally distributed across multiple steps: a wrong tool choice in step 2 leads to a bad result in step 5 that produces a harmful output in step 7. The blast radius of a reasoning error is larger.
If an agent is retried after a partial failure, earlier tool calls may execute twice. Design tools to be idempotent — or record which tools ran and skip them on retry.
The execution path is known at design time, so audit logging can be added at each step systematically. Agent audit logs must capture the full message history to be interpretable.
Prompt injection is more dangerous in agents.
A workflow calls tools in a fixed order regardless of tool output content. An agent reads tool output to decide its next action — malicious content in a tool result can steer subsequent decisions.
Workflows need timeouts. Agents need step limits. Both need circuit breakers that abort runs consuming disproportionate resources.
Think of a workflow as a

. A recipe lists steps 1 through 8 and the cook follows them in order. The recipe handles any standard execution of the dish. But if the oven is broken, an ingredient is missing, or the guest has an allergy the recipe doesn't account for, a cook with judgment can adapt; a rigid recipe follower cannot.

Most dishes are best made with a recipe: it's faster, cheaper, and consistent. But a restaurant kitchen that can only execute pre-written recipes will fail the moment conditions deviate from normal. The chef (agent) is more expensive to employ and less predictable, but indispensable for the situations the recipe doesn't cover. Both belong in the kitchen — the question is which to reach for first.
The defining difference: in a workflow, the developer controls the execution graph; in an agent, the LLM controls it at runtime.
Both can use LLMs and tools. The distinction is not capability but control flow ownership.
Default to workflows. Add an agent loop only when the task requires dynamic step selection that cannot be encoded in advance.
Workflows have fixed, predictable costs. Agent costs are a distribution — budget for the tail, not the median.
Workflows fail locally and predictably. Agents fail emergently across multiple steps, which makes diagnosis harder.
Hybrid architectures are common: a workflow outer shell with an agent embedded as one step localizes non-determinism to where it adds value.
Prompt injection is more dangerous in agents because malicious tool output influences subsequent tool choices — not just the current output.
